name: Nuu-chah-nulth
language: Nuu-chah-nulth (a dialect continuum; 14 distinct First Nations with related languages)
traditionalTerritories: The west coast of Vancouver Island from Brooks Peninsula in the north to Port Renfrew in the south, British Columbia. Each of the 14 Nuu-chah-nulth nations has distinct traditional territories.
culturalNotes: Nuu-chah-nulth means 'all along the mountains and the sea' — a description of their Pacific coast environment on the exposed western shore of Vancouver Island. The Nuu-chah-nulth include 14 distinct First Nations, each with their own governance, territorial boundaries, and cultural protocols. The Wakashan language family is entirely distinct from Coast Salish — a reminder that 'Pacific Northwest Coast' encompasses multiple unrelated language families. Representative member nations include Ahousaht, Ucluelet, Tla-o-qui-aht, and Hesquiaht among others. Nuu-chah-nulth were among the primary whale hunters of the Northwest Coast. In the 1990s, the Nuu-chah-nulth brought a landmark Aboriginal title and rights case that significantly shaped Canadian Aboriginal law.
heritageSkillRelevance: Ocean-going dugout canoe construction — Nuu-chah-nulth canoes were traded throughout the Pacific Coast
Whale hunting — ceremonially complex tradition; protocols are Level 3-4; historical techniques are Level 1-2
Halibut fishing using traditional hooks, line, and kelp anchor systems
Cedar bark weaving — hats, baskets, cloaks, rope; Nuu-chah-nulth cedar hat is an iconic Pacific Coast form
Red cedar harvesting with appropriate thanksgiving protocols
Dried salmon and herring preservation for winter and trade
Eulachon (oolichan) oil extraction and use — grease trade connections to interior peoples
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Nuu-chah-nulth is a Wakashan language family — entirely distinct from Coast Salish. The 14 member nations each have distinct protocols. Do not aggregate all 14 nations as 'Nuu-chah-nulth' without acknowledging individual nation identities; for specific content, name the specific nation (Ahousaht, Ucluelet, Tla-o-qui-aht, Hesquiaht, etc.). Whale hunting ceremonial content is Level 3-4. Public technique history is Level 1-2. Potlatch-specific ceremonial knowledge is distinct from publicly shared cultural practices and techniques.

name: Kwakwaka'wakw (Kwakiutl)
traditionalTerritories: Northern Vancouver Island, the Broughton Archipelago, and the adjacent British Columbia mainland coast. Traditional territory includes Alert Bay, Port Hardy, Campbell River area, and the mainland coastal inlets.
culturalNotes: Kwakwaka'wakw means 'those who speak Kwak'wala' — a name that emphasizes linguistic and cultural unity across multiple distinct groups including Namgis, Kwakiutl, Mamalilikala, and Wei Wai Kum. The Kwakwaka'wakw are known for the most elaborately documented potlatch tradition on the Northwest Coast — the 1922 Cape Mudge/Alert Bay potlatch raid by Canadian authorities and the subsequent confiscation of regalia is a central event in Northwest Coast cultural history. The U'mista Cultural Centre in Alert Bay holds much of the repatriated regalia from that raid. Kwak'wala is in the Southern Wakashan branch, distinct from Northern Wakashan (Heiltsuk, Haisla) and Nootkan Wakashan (Nuu-chah-nulth, Makah).
heritageSkillRelevance: Totem pole carving — Kwakwaka'wakw pole traditions are documented in major museum collections; protocols are Level 2-3 depending on family ownership
Potlatch as social technology — the publicly shared gift economy mechanics (Level 1); ceremonial details are Level 3-4
Kwak'wala language revitalization (U'mista Cultural Centre programs)
Dugout canoe construction — Northern Vancouver Island cedar
Cedar bark weaving and cedar rope production
Ceremonial regalia carving and painting — family and clan ownership applies (Level 2-3)
Salmon and eulachon (grease trail) food traditions
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Kwakwaka'wakw encompasses multiple distinct groups (Namgis, Kwakiutl, Mamalilikala, Wei Wai Kum, and others) — name the specific group when the context is specific. Wakashan language family is distinct from Coast Salish. Potlatch ceremonial content is Level 3-4; publicly shared cultural technology framing is Level 1. Totem pole designs carry family/clan ownership at Level 2-3. The 1922 potlatch confiscation and repatriation history is essential context for any potlatch content.

name: Heiltsuk (Bella Bella)
traditionalTerritories: Central BC coast including Bella Bella (Waglisla), King Island, Campbell Island, and the surrounding islands and mainland inlets of the central coast.
culturalNotes: Heiltsuk traditional territory is on the remote central BC coast — one of the most ecologically rich coastal environments remaining in Canada. Heiltsuk have maintained significant traditional practices including herring spawn harvesting, which has been central to multiple landmark legal cases asserting Aboriginal title and fisheries rights. The Heiltsuk Nation's clam garden restoration project is a model for Indigenous-led coastal ecosystem management.
heritageSkillRelevance: Herring spawn on kelp harvesting — a central Heiltsuk food and trade item; methods are publicly documented
Clam garden construction and management — archaeological evidence of ancient cultivation practices
Dugout canoe construction and navigation of the central coast inlets
Heiltsuk language revitalization programs
Cedar bark weaving — central coast variant traditions
Eulachon (grease) harvesting and oil rendering
Marine mammal relationships — historical and contemporary observational practices
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Heiltsuk is a distinct Northern Wakashan language and people — distinct from Southern Wakashan Kwakwaka'wakw and from Coast Salish. 'Bella Bella' is a geographic label; the preferred name is Heiltsuk or Haíɫzaqv. Potlatch-specific ceremonial content is Level 3-4; publicly shared cultural techniques are Level 1-2.

name: Nuxalk (Bella Coola)
languageFamily: Salishan (isolated — Bella Coola Salishan, not Northwest Coast Salishan)
language: Nuxalk (Bella Coola Salish — an isolated Salishan language surrounded by Wakashan territory)
traditionalTerritories: Bella Coola River valley and the head of Burke Channel, British Columbia — a river valley reaching from the Pacific coast deep into the Coast Mountains.
culturalNotes: The Nuxalk occupy a linguistically remarkable position: they speak a Salishan language yet are geographically and culturally embedded among Wakashan-speaking peoples (Heiltsuk, Kwakwaka'wakw). The Bella Coola River is the first major salmon river north of the Fraser system, and the Nuxalk traditional economy was built on the exceptional salmon resources of this fjord-river ecosystem. Alexander Mackenzie reached the Pacific in 1793 via Nuxalk territory — the first documented overland European crossing of North America above Mexico.
heritageSkillRelevance: Bella Coola River salmon fishing — chinook runs at the head of North Bentinck Arm
Eulachon (oolichan) grease rendering — Bella Coola was a significant grease trade node
Dugout canoe construction for Burke Channel and fjord navigation
Interior mountain resource harvesting via the Bella Coola River corridor
Nuxalk language revitalization efforts
Cedar bark processing and weaving within a Wakashan cultural context
Grease trail trade network participation — connecting coast to interior
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Nuxalk is a Salishan language family but culturally distinct from Northwest Coast Salishan peoples — their geographic and cultural position is among Wakashan neighbors. Do not aggregate with Coast Salish peoples. Ceremonial content (the Kusiut and Sisaok ceremonial systems) is Level 3-4.

name: Haisla Nation
language: Haisla (Northern Wakashan — closely related to Heiltsuk)
traditionalTerritories: Douglas Channel and the Kitimat Arm, British Columbia, and the surrounding fjords and inlets at the head of the coast mountain fjord system.
culturalNotes: Haisla Nation territory is at the head of Douglas Channel — a long, deep fjord that penetrates the Coast Mountains nearly to the Skeena watershed. The Haisla have been at the center of some of the most significant BC environmental conflicts of the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries, particularly regarding pipeline and LNG terminal proposals in their territory. The Haisla language is one of the most endangered languages on the Northwest Coast, with active revitalization efforts underway.
heritageSkillRelevance: Eulachon (oolichan) harvesting at the Kitimat River — one of the largest eulachon runs remaining
Douglas Channel and fjord salmon fishing — chinook and coho in deep fjord channels
Dugout canoe construction and navigation of deep-water fjords
Haisla language revitalization programs
Cedar bark harvesting and weaving traditions
Mountain goat relationships and mountain resource harvesting via fjord-mountain interface
Grease trail connections via the Douglas Channel-Skeena corridor
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Haisla is a Northern Wakashan language — distinct from both Southern Wakashan (Kwakwaka'wakw) and from Coast Salish. Do not conflate with Heiltsuk despite linguistic relationship. Ceremonial content is Level 3-4.

name: Tsimshian (Sm'algyax-speaking peoples)
languageFamily: Tsimshianic (a distinct family — not Wakashan or Salishan)
traditionalTerritories: Skeena River mouth, Prince Rupert Harbour, and the adjacent islands and mainland coast of northern BC. Three distinct Tsimshian communities: Kitsumkalum and Kitselas (Skeena River valley), and Metlakatla (Prince Rupert area).
culturalNotes: The Tsimshian are a distinct linguistic and cultural family — Sm'algyax is unrelated to both Wakashan and Salishan languages. The Tsimshian occupied the mouth of the Skeena River, one of the major Pacific salmon rivers of northern BC, and the extraordinary fish wealth of the Skeena sustained large permanent villages and a stratified society with ranked clans, hereditary chiefs, and a complex ceremony system. The Tsimshian were important trade intermediaries between the coast and the Skeena interior (Gitxsan, Wet'suwet'en). Three federally recognized Tsimshian communities include Kitsumkalum (at Terrace), Kitselas (in the Skeena canyon), and Metlakatla (on the coast near Prince Rupert).
heritageSkillRelevance: Skeena River salmon fishing — among the most abundant salmon rivers in northern BC
Eulachon (oolichan) harvesting and grease rendering at the Nass River (adjacent to Tsimshian territory)
Dugout canoe construction for Prince Rupert Harbour and coastal travel
Sm'algyax language revitalization efforts
Totem pole traditions — Tsimshian poles are among the most documented in museum collections; family ownership applies at Level 2-3
Clan and house systems — publicly documented structure (Level 1-2); specific ceremony is Level 3-4
Chilkat-adjacent blanket weaving traditions (distinct from Tlingit Chilkat though related)
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Tsimshian is a distinct language family — not Wakashan, not Salishan, not Athapaskan. Name specific communities (Kitsumkalum, Kitselas, Metlakatla) when context is specific. Totem pole design carries family and clan ownership at Level 2-3. Ceremonial content is Level 3-4. Potlatch protocols are distinct from publicly shared social technology framing.
